name: Piskies
description: Piskies, also known as pixies or piskeys, are mischievous sprites from Cornish folklore. They are generally depicted as small, humanoid creatures with pointed ears and often green or brown clothing. Piskies are known for their playful tricks, which can range from harmless pranks to leading travelers astray. They are said to inhabit ancient sites, such as stone circles, barrows, and caves, as well as natural features like streams and woodlands. While not inherently malevolent, Piskies are easily offended and can become vengeful if disrespected. They are associated with luck, both good and bad, and are believed to possess knowledge of hidden treasures and the secrets of the land. Offerings of cream or other treats are sometimes left out to appease them. Stories of Piskies often serve as cautionary tales, warning against greed, disrespect for nature, and straying from established paths. They are an integral part of Cornwall's rich cultural heritage, representing the enduring belief in the magical realm that coexists with the everyday world. Their mischievous nature and connection to the land make them a captivating and enduring figure in Cornish mythology.
subtitle: Mischievous sprites of Cornish folklore
appearance: Piskies are typically described as small, humanoid creatures, ranging in size from a few inches to about two feet tall. They have pointed ears, often said to be like those of an elf or a fox. Their clothing is usually made from natural materials, such as leaves, moss, or animal skins, and is often green or brown in color. Some accounts depict them with wrinkled faces and mischievous eyes. While some Piskies are described as being beautiful, others are said to be more grotesque or comical in appearance. Some tales describe them as having red hair, while others depict them as having dark, mischievous eyes. They are often associated with a faint, ethereal glow.
habitat: Piskies are said to inhabit a variety of natural locations in Cornwall, including ancient sites such as stone circles, barrows, and caves. They are also found in woodlands, streams, and other natural features. They are particularly associated with areas of wild, untamed beauty. Some stories suggest that they live in underground caverns or hidden glades.
behavior: Piskies are known for their mischievous and playful behavior. They enjoy playing pranks on humans, such as tangling their hair, hiding their belongings, or leading them astray. While not inherently malevolent, they can be easily offended and may become vengeful if disrespected. They are also said to be fond of music and dancing, and will sometimes gather in secret glades to celebrate. Piskies are associated with luck, both good and bad, and are believed to possess knowledge of hidden treasures and the secrets of the land. They are often seen as guardians of the natural world.

keyMyths.title: Piskie-Led
keyMyths.description: Travelers who lose their way in Cornwall are often said to be 'Piskie-led,' meaning they have been intentionally misled by the mischievous sprites. The only way to escape this predicament is to turn your coat inside out or sit down and wait for the Piskies to lose interest.

keyMyths.title: The Piskie's Treasure
keyMyths.description: Many tales tell of Piskies guarding hidden treasures in Cornwall, often buried beneath ancient stones or in secret caves. However, attempting to steal this treasure is said to bring bad luck or the wrath of the Piskies upon the thief.

sources.author: Robert Hunt
sources.work: Popular Romances of the West of England
sources.description: A collection of Cornish folklore, including numerous tales of Piskies and their antics.
sources.text: A collection of Cornish folklore, including numerous tales of Piskies and their antics.

sources.author: William Bottrell
